request.question: How many venues are involved in the main tournament which is part of the English Cricket Team's 2017-2018 schedule?

title: 2017–18 Ashes series
Betty Archdale
Centenary Test
Bob Wyatt

English cricket team in India in 2005–06

English cricket team in Australia in 2017–18
sentences: The 2017–18 Ashes series is a forthcoming series of Test cricket matches to be contested between England and Australia for The Ashes.
The series will be played at five venues across Australia between 23 November 2017 and 8 January 2018.
Helen Elizabeth "Betty" Archdale (21 August 1907 – 11 January 2000) was an educationalist and cricketer.
She was a captain of the English women's cricket team in 1934 and 1935.
In 1934/35 she led the first English cricket team to tour Australia and New Zealand, the result of which was a 2-0 victory over Australia.
This tour did much both to raise the status of women's cricket and to heal some of the damage done to Anglo-Australian cricket relations by bodyline two years earlier.
Centenary Test refers to two matches of Test cricket played between the English cricket team and the Australian cricket team, the first in 1977 and the second in 1980.
These matches were played to mark the 100th anniversaries of the first Test cricket matches played in Australia (1877) and in England (1880) respectively.
Neither match was played for The Ashes.
Robert "Bob" Elliott Storey Wyatt (2 May 1901, Milford Heath House, Surrey, England – 20 April 1995, Treliske, Truro, Cornwall) was an English cricket player.
He played for Warwickshire, Worcestershire, and the English cricket team.
Before the beginning of the 2005 English cricket season began, a resurgent English cricket team had won four Test series in a row, going unbeaten through the 2004 calendar year.
The start of the international season saw England defeat Bangladesh 2–0 in their two-match series, winning both Tests by an innings.
This was followed by a tri-nations one-day tournament that also featured Australia.
However, all eyes were on the Ashes.
Australia still started as favourites, but England, for the first time in years, were expected to put up a real challenge to Aussie supremacy.
The English cricket team toured India during February, March and April 2006.

The English cricket team was aspiring to maintain the form that took them to second place in the ICC Test Championship before their disastrous spell against Pakistan, and which helped win the 2005 Ashes series at home to Australia.

This goal was substantially hindered by the usual stomach complaints which nearly always dog the English team in the opening weeks of Indian tours, and a recurrence of an injury to the captain Michael Vaughan; the swing bowler Simon Jones and the absence of Ashley Giles who missed the tour for an operation.

As well as this, stand-in captain Marcus Trescothick flew home for "personal reasons", not wishing to divulge further, leaving Andrew Flintoff, who missed the birth of his son, to take on the title of skipper for the first time having to captain two maiden international cricketers on the English side: Alastair Cook and Monty Panesar as well as Shanthakumaran Sreesanth, Piyush Chawla and Munaf Patel for the home team.

International cricket played in the 2006 cricket season is defined as matches scheduled between May and August 2006 in all cricketing countries, as well as all international matches scheduled for the 2006 English cricket season.
Matches between January and April are defined as belonging to the 2005–06 season, while matches between September and December are defined as the 2006–07 season.

The main matches in this period were played in England, as this was in the middle of the English cricket season, but the third edition of the ICC Intercontinental Cup is defined as a part of the 2006 season, even though the tournament will stretch into February 2007, and three A teams are also scheduled to tour the northwestern part of Australia in June and July 2006.
The Barmy Army is a semi-organised group of English cricket fans which arranges touring parties for some of its members to follow the English cricket team on overseas tours.
The name is also applied to followers of the team who join in with match day activities in the crowd, but do not necessarily travel as part of an organised tour.
The 2004 English cricket season saw the English cricket team record a 4-0 Test series whitewash over the West Indies and a comfortable 3-0 win over New Zealand.
Their one-day form was sporadic, however.
In the Natwest Trophy, they failed to make the final, which saw New Zealand defeat the West Indies by 107 runs.
In the Natwest Challenge, they beat India 2-1.
In domestic cricket, Warwickshire won the County Championship.
The England cricket team is scheduled to tour Australia between November 2017 and February 2018 to play five Tests and five One Day Internationals (ODIs).
They will also take part in a three nation Twenty20 International (T20I) tournament, along with New Zealand, who will co-host the tournament along with Australia.
The Test series will form part of the 2017–18 Ashes series.
In May 2017, it was confirmed that the WACA Ground would host the Test and ODI in Perth, as the planned new ground was unable to open in time.

title: English cricket team in Australia in 2017–18
2017–18 Ashes series
2017–18 Ashes series

candidate.summarize1.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `passages`, produce the fields `summary`.

You are the first-hop summarizer. Compress the supplied passages into a short, actionable intermediate state that lets the next agent know exactly what is present in the passages, what is missing, and what to fetch next. Produce a single `summary` string only.

Summary content requirements (concise: a few short sentences or clauses):
- First-hop evidence: one short paraphrase or a very brief quoted phrase from the passages that links the relevant entities or facts supporting any partial answer. Do not copy long verbatim spans.
- Bridge entity: explicitly name the person/entity mentioned in the passages that the second hop should investigate (use the full name or label exactly as it appears in the passages). If the question uses only a given name and the passages do not disambiguate which individual is meant, state clearly that the identity is missing/ambiguous.
- Missing fact: state in one short sentence which specific fact is required to answer the question (for example: winner name, year, birthplace, or another identifying detail) and confirm it is not present in the given passages.
- Retrieval directions: give one concise, ordered, actionable next-hop plan (1–2 steps). Prefer concrete target pages or query patterns, e.g. "open the named person's biography to check for [missing fact]" or a short query pattern such as '"<NAME>" birthplace OR "born in" site:wikipedia.org' or 'open a chronological list of <AWARD> winners and search for <NAME>'. Do not instruct the system to execute retrieval here — only specify what the next retrieval should fetch.
- Clarification: if ambiguity persists (e.g., only a given name is provided and multiple candidates are possible), include a short clarifying request asking the user to provide the full name.

Hard constraints and tone:
- Be concise and factual. Keep the same neutral, summary tone as before.
- Do not guess, infer, or assert any facts not present in the passages (no candidate answers, no years, no birthplace assertions, etc.).
- Do not ask the user for permission to search external sources. Give explicit next-hop retrieval directions instead.
- Output only the single `summary` field text complying with the above requirements.
candidate.create_query_hop2.predict: Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, produce the fields `query`.
candidate.summarize2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `context`, `passages`, produce the fields `summary`.

You are integrating newly retrieved passages (second‑hop evidence) with the first‑hop context to produce a grounded, post‑retrieval summary that preserves the facts the final answer generator will need. Produce a single field `summary` whose value is 1–3 concise sentences that: (1) directly state the answer to the question (e.g., where the studios/recordings were located) based on the available evidence; (2) if any passage explicitly names a production facility or studio and ties that facility to a specific locality (for example by giving the facility name together with a street, town, or county), include one short supporting sentence that paraphrases that passage to show the link between the facility and the locality; and (3) include one very short sentence or clause that synthesizes the context and the supporting passage to place the studios in the stated locality. Paraphrase supporting passages; do not include long verbatim quotations.

If the passages do not conclusively link a named studio to a specific locality, preserve and state that uncertainty rather than asserting unsupported specifics. Avoid headers, meta commentary, long quotations, extra sections, or inventing details not present in the provided context/passages. Keep phrasing compact, factual, and focused on the evidence needed to support the final answer.

Output contract: produce only the `summary` field (1–3 concise sentences) that integrates the first‑hop context with the second‑hop passages as described above.
candidate.final_answer.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, and `summary_2`, produce the field `answer` as follows. Use only the visible summaries (summary_1 and summary_2) as the evidential basis for the final answer; do not add external facts except where the instructions below explicitly allow a limited, well‑justified use of internal background knowledge. Always produce the final result inside the [[ ## answer ## ]] block and make that block contain only the minimal answer string required by these rules.

1) General minimal-answer rule
- If the summaries unambiguously support a single short answer (a short name, title, demonym, year, or single noun/phrase of roughly 1–5 words), emit only that minimal token sequence as the answer. Normalize the chosen substring by removing any leading/trailing whitespace or surrounding punctuation but preserve the phrase's internal characters and capitalization exactly as shown in the summaries. Do not add quotes, em dashes, braces, labels, trailing punctuation, explanatory clauses, or any additional text.

2) When both summaries agree on an identical short phrase
- If summary_1 and summary_2 contain the same short named-entity string (after lowercasing only for agreement detection), extract that common phrase, strip surrounding punctuation/whitespace, and emit it verbatim (preserving original capitalization) as the sole content of [[ ## answer ## ]] with no surrounding or appended characters.

3) Patterned question triggers (minimal formatting + deterministic selection)
- 'Which ... is from X, A or B?': When the question asks which of two named options is from a place and the summaries clearly indicate a single band/person as the answer, emit only the minimal band/person name supported by the summaries.
- Comparative membership questions (e.g., 'Does A or B have more band members?'): If the summaries clearly indicate which band has more members, emit only that band's name.
- Short common-name questions (e.g., 'What do people in the UK commonly call the location ...?'): If both summaries provide the same short common name, emit only that short name string.

4) Nationality questions
- If the question asks for a nationality/demonym and either summary_1 or summary_2 contains a demonym, emit only the minimal demonym token/phrase. Prefer the first demonym found in summary_1; if none appears in summary_1, use the first found in summary_2. Emit it verbatim (no added words or punctuation).

5) Two-performer composition specificity
- If the question asks for the "type of musical composition" used by a named act and both summaries use a generic word like 'song(s)', you may consult internal knowledge only to determine whether the act is a two-person (duo) group. If you can reliably confirm the act is a two-person group, replace the generic term with the single-word or very short noun that names a composition intended for two performers (for example, 'duet') and emit that single word as the minimal answer. If you cannot reliably confirm duo membership, emit the minimal noun exactly as it appears in the summaries.

6) Narrow assumed referent (single-name trigger)
- If the question supplies only a given name (e.g., 'Maurice') and the visible summaries explicitly mention a specific example with that given name (e.g., a named example appears in the summaries) and the summaries identify the requested fact (e.g., the award), then you may assume that referent and emit the minimal answer (for example, a single year) if you have sufficient internal confidence. If you do not have sufficient internal confidence, follow the normal clarification behavior (emit a brief clarifying question as the answer field) rather than guessing.

7) What to avoid (formatting and content prohibitions)
- Never add explanatory text, full sentences, comparisons, justifications, or provenance in the answer field. Do not append decorative punctuation (em dashes, extra commas, trailing periods), quotes, braces, JSON-like structures, leading newlines, or any characters not part of the normalized minimal phrase. Do not invent facts not present in the summaries except where the two narrowly scoped rules above permit limited internal confirmation (duo-confirmation and the single-name assumed referent). If the summaries do not unambiguously support a single minimal answer and you lack permitted internal justification, emit a concise clarification request as the answer.

Additional specific, evidence-directed selection rules (apply these only when their trigger conditions are met, otherwise follow the rules above):

8) Prefer an explicitly named immediate jurisdiction when resolving county/country wording conflicts
- Trigger: The question asks which country/sovereign-state a county or borough is part of (phrasing like 'county' and 'part of what country' or equivalent) and the summaries disagree or one summary uses a parenthetical membership note such as 'A (part of B)'.
- Behavior: If one summary contains a parenthetical of the form 'A (part of B)', treat the pre-parenthetical name A as the immediate administrative region referenced by the summaries and emit A (trimmed of surrounding punctuation/whitespace) as the minimal answer. More generally, when summaries disagree about a place, prefer the summary that directly names the immediate jurisdiction associated with the county (the subnational or constituent area named in the summary) and extract that short named phrase verbatim as the answer. Do not add any extra words or punctuation.
- Fallback: If neither summary clearly names an immediate jurisdiction or both are equally ambiguous, emit a concise clarification question per the base rules rather than guessing.

9) Size-answer simplification when both summaries agree with an inclusive qualifier
- Trigger: The question explicitly asks for a size (phrasing like 'what size' or 'equal to or smaller than what size' or equivalent) AND summary_1 and summary_2 are identical strings for agreement-detection (case-insensitive) AND that common string matches the pattern '<size> or smaller' or '<size> or less' (case-insensitive, allowing optional surrounding punctuation/whitespace).
- Behavior: Produce as the minimal answer only the '<size>' portion of the agreed common phrase by removing the trailing inclusive qualifier 'or smaller' or 'or less' and any immediately adjacent punctuation/extra whitespace. Emit the remaining size token (number plus unit) verbatim as shown in the summaries (preserve digits, unit spelling/capitalization, and internal spacing).
- Do not apply this edit when the summaries do not exactly agree, when the common phrase does not match the pattern, or when the question is not asking for a size.

10) Prefer identical parenthetical short-names when present in both summaries
- Trigger: Both summary_1 and summary_2 each contain an identical parenthetical short-name substring (a substring of the form '(' ... ')' that, once trimmed, is 1–5 words long, agreement checked case-insensitively).
- Behavior: Extract the parenthetical content (remove the surrounding '(' and ')', trim surrounding whitespace and surrounding punctuation) and treat that extracted short-name as the canonical minimal answer. Emit it verbatim (preserving internal characters and capitalization exactly as shown in the summaries) as the sole content of [[ ## answer ## ]]. Do not emit the longer formal name when such an identical parenthetical is present in both summaries.

Output contract reminder: The produced `answer` must be exactly the minimal answer string determined by the rules above (or a short clarification question when required). The [[ ## answer ## ]] block must contain only that string and nothing else.

gold_support_titles: English cricket team in Australia in 2017–18
response.hop1_query: How many venues are involved in the main tournament which is part of the English Cricket Team's 2017-2018 schedule?
hop1_docs: English cricket team in Australia in 2017–18 | The England cricket team is scheduled to tour Australia between November 2017 and February 2018 to play five Tests and five One Day Internationals (ODIs).  They will also take part in a three nation Twenty20 International (T20I) tournament, along with New Zealand, who will co-host the tournament along with Australia.  The Test series will form part of the 2017–18 Ashes series.  In May 2017, it was confirmed that the WACA Ground would host the Test and ODI in Perth, as the planned new ground was unable to open in time.

2017–18 Regional Super50 | The 2017–18 Regional Super50 is scheduled to be the 44th edition of the Regional Super50, the domestic limited-overs cricket competition for the countries of the West Indies Cricket Board (WICB).  The tournament is scheduled to be held in early 2018, with the English county sides Kent and Hampshire being invited to take part in the competition.  Kent took part in the previous edition of the tournament.  Barbados are the defending champions.

2018 ICC World Cricket League Division Four | The 2018 ICC World Cricket League Division Four is an international cricket tournament that is scheduled to take place during mid-2018.  It will form part of the 2017–22 cycle of the World Cricket League (WCL) which determines the qualification for the 2023 Cricket World Cup.

2018 ICC World Cricket League Division Two | 2018 ICC World Cricket League Division Two is a cricket tournament that is scheduled to take place in 2018.  Oman and Canada were the top two teams in the 2017 ICC World Cricket League Division Three tournament in Uganda and were promoted as a result.  They will be joined by the bottom four teams from the ongoing 2015–17 ICC World Cricket League Championship to determine the final two spots in the 2018 Cricket World Cup Qualifier.

2017–18 Pac-12 Conference men's basketball season | The 2017–18 Pac-12 Conference men's basketball season begins with practices in October 2017 followed by the 2017–18 NCAA Division I men's basketball season in November 10, 2017 with the conference schedule will begin on December 27, 2017.  The 2018 Pac-12 Tournament in March 2018 at the T-Mobile Arena in Paradise, Nevada.  Due to the Pac-12's use of that venue for their conference tournament, the Pac-12 Tournament will take place one week earlier than usual, ending the week before Selection Sunday.  This could result in teams having nearly two weeks off before the 2018 NCAA tournament.  As a result, it is anticipated that the Pac-12 regular season will begin in late-December.  Pac-12 teams' first Pac-12 game would likely be on the weekend of December, 2017 with a second game on December , 2017.  Coaches have requested that Pac-12 game be scheduled between Christmas and New Year's Day, accordingly each team will play two conference games in early December before finishing non-conference play.

2017–18 Pro50 Championship | The 2017–18 Pro50 Championship is scheduled to be the fifteenth edition of the Pro50 Championship, a List A cricket tournament in Zimbabwe.  The competition will run from December 2017 to March 2018 and will include a new team, the Rising Stars, along with the existing four teams.  Matabeleland Tuskers are the defending champions.

2018 ICC Women's World Twenty20 | The 2018 ICC Women's World Twenty20 will be hosted in the West Indies from 2 to 25 November 2018, during the 2018–19 international cricket season.  It will be the sixth edition of the ICC Women's World Twenty20, and the second hosted by the West Indies (after the 2010 edition).  The tournament was awarded to the West Indies Cricket Board (WICB) at the 2013 annual conference of the International Cricket Council (ICC).  The tournament's dates were confirmed at an ICC board meeting in January 2015.  In February 2017, the ICC confirmed that this would be the first T20 tournament that uses the Umpire Decision Review System, with one review per side.  The qualifier tournament for the competition is scheduled to be held in late 2017 or early 2018.

response.summary_1: Evidence: Passage [1] says England "will also take part in a three nation Twenty20 International (T20I) tournament" co-hosted by New Zealand and Australia. Bridge entity: "three nation Twenty20 International (T20I) tournament" (as named in the passages). Missing fact: the specific number of venues used by that main tournament is not given in the supplied passages. Retrieval directions: 1) Open the "English cricket team in Australia in 2017–18" article and/or the dedicated page for the named three-nation T20I tournament (tri-series) and check the "Venues" or "Matches" section for a count/list of grounds; or 2) search for '"England New Zealand Australia tri-series 2017" venues site:wikipedia.org' to locate the tournament page and extract the number of venues. Clarification: If by "main tournament" you meant a different event on England's 2017–18 schedule, please specify the tournament name.
response.hop2_query: Open the Wikipedia page for the 2017–18 Trans-Tasman/Tri-series (England, Australia, New Zealand) — or the "English cricket team in Australia in 2017–18" article — and report the number of venues listed in the tournament's "Venues" or "Matches" section. (Search: "England New Zealand Australia tri-series 2017 venues site:wikipedia.org")
hop2_docs: 2017–18 Trans-Tasman Tri-Series | The 2017–18 Trans-Tasman Tri-Series is an upcoming Twenty20 International (T20I) cricket tournament scheduled to be held in Australia and New Zealand in February 2018.  It is a tri-nation series between Australia, England and New Zealand.  It follows on from England's tour of Australia, which includes the 2017–18 Ashes series, and takes the place of the planned Chappell–Hadlee Trophy series.

English cricket team in Australia in 2017–18 | The England cricket team is scheduled to tour Australia between November 2017 and February 2018 to play five Tests and five One Day Internationals (ODIs).  They will also take part in a three nation Twenty20 International (T20I) tournament, along with New Zealand, who will co-host the tournament along with Australia.  The Test series will form part of the 2017–18 Ashes series.  In May 2017, it was confirmed that the WACA Ground would host the Test and ODI in Perth, as the planned new ground was unable to open in time.

2017–18 Ashes series | The 2017–18 Ashes series is a forthcoming series of Test cricket matches to be contested between England and Australia for The Ashes.  The series will be played at five venues across Australia between 23 November 2017 and 8 January 2018.

English cricket team in Australia in 2006–07 | The England cricket team tour of Australia included the 2006–07 Ashes series, a number of first-class matches against Australian Domestic teams, participation in the tri-nation One-day International series between Australia, New Zealand and England and a Twenty20 match against Australia on 9 January 2007.

2017 Ireland Tri-Nation Series | The 2017 Ireland Tri-Nation Series was a One Day International cricket tournament that took place in Ireland in May 2017.  It was a tri-nation series between Ireland, Bangladesh and New Zealand.  The matches were in preparation for the 2017 ICC Champions Trophy, which is scheduled to take place in June 2017 in England and Wales.  Cricket Ireland announced the full fixtures in July 2016.  Ahead of the ODI fixtures, Ireland played two warm-up matches; a 50-over match against Bangladesh and a 25-over match against New Zealand.

1982–83 Australian Tri-Series | The 1982-83 World Series was a One Day International (ODI) cricket tri-series where Australia played host to England and New Zealand.  Australia and New Zealand reached the Finals, which Australia won 3-0.

1990–91 Australian Tri-Series | The 1990–91 World Series was a One Day International (ODI) cricket tri-series where Australia played host to England and New Zealand.  Australia and New Zealand reached the Finals, which Australia won 2-0.
